content.text: for agent teams are a codified set of requirements that an engineering team must satisfy before their agent service is granted access to the shared observability platform. These standards typically specify: which span attributes are mandatory, what SLO declarations must be made, how alert routing must be configured, what PII and PHI safeguards must be in place, and what automated validation gates must pass in CI before a service enters production. The standards serve a dual purpose: they protect the shared platform from schema drift and cardinality abuse, and they guarantee that every onboarded service is observable from day one without manual platform team intervention.

Without onboarding standards, a new agent team joining the platform will instrument however seems convenient — using arbitrary attribute names, omitting required fields, or accidentally logging PII. The resulting traces are incompatible with organisation-wide dashboards, cannot be correlated across services, may violate compliance requirements, and inflate cardinality in the shared Prometheus. At the other extreme, teams that skip instrumentation entirely enter production invisible — incidents go undetected until customers report them. Onboarding standards prevent both failure modes by making correctness a precondition for platform access, enforced by CI gates rather than manual review.
Every agent service that completes onboarding is queryable in the shared trace backend, dashboardable with the standard template, and has at least one SLO defined.
Automated CI gates move schema compliance from a reviewer concern to a build gate, preventing schema drift at merge time.
Consistent mandatory attributes (service.name, service.team) enable organisation-wide cross-service correlation queries.
Compliance checks at onboarding catch PII in span attributes before the service ever writes to the compliance archive.
SLO declarations at onboarding force teams to have the reliability conversation before shipping, not after the first incident.
Upfront friction: teams must satisfy all checklist items before their service is observable, which adds days to initial deployment timelines.
Standards require maintenance: as the platform evolves, onboarding requirements change, and existing tenants may need retroactive updates.
Automated lint tools must understand domain-specific attribute semantics (e.g., that llm.model should be a string, not an integer) — generic schema validators miss this.
Onboarding process can become a bureaucratic gate rather than an enabler if the platform team is slow to process registrations.

Onboarding follows a five-step flow: (1) the team registers their service in the platform's service registry (a YAML file in a shared git repo); (2) they adopt the platform SDK in their application code; (3) CI validates that all required span attributes are present in staging traces using a span schema linter; (4) the team declares their SLOs in a
file that the platform reads to configure Grafana and alerting; (5) the platform provisions their namespace and dashboard template, and the service enters production.

The span schema linter runs as a CI step against staging traces captured during integration tests. It checks that required attributes are present on root spans, that attribute types match the schema, that no banned attribute patterns (e.g., attributes containing raw user input) are present, and that the compliance scope attribute is set if the service processes regulated data.

attrs.alt: Tenant onboarding flow: register, SDK adoption, schema check, SLO config, go live; with required attributes, SLO declarations, and CI validation gates

content.text: The five-step onboarding flow begins with service registration and ends with a provisioned dashboard and active alerts. Three categories of requirements — required span attributes, SLO declarations, and CI validation gates — must be satisfied before the platform grants the service access to shared infrastructure.
Any organisation with a shared observability platform serving multiple agent engineering teams.
When onboarding new agent services more than twice per quarter — ad-hoc manual review does not scale.
When compliance requirements mandate that all agent services pass instrumentation review before touching regulated data.
When past incidents reveal that some services went to production without proper instrumentation, causing delayed detection.
Define the required attribute schema in a machine-readable format (JSON Schema or OTel semantic convention YAML). Store it in the shared platform repository.
Build a span schema linter as a CLI tool that accepts an OTLP JSON export from staging and validates it against the required schema.
Add the linter as a required CI step in the platform's shared CI template that all agent service pipelines extend.
Create a service registry YAML file format and a pull-request-based registration workflow where new teams submit their registration.
Automate namespace provisioning in Grafana and alert routing in PagerDuty from the service registry using a GitOps controller.
Publish an onboarding guide with a worked example and a self-service portal for teams to check their lint status before submitting.

# service-registry/customer-experience/customer-support-agent.yaml
service:
  name: customer-support-agent
  team: customer-experience
  version: 2.4.1
  environment: production
  owner_email: team-cx@company.com
  pagerduty_key: abc123def456
  compliance_scope: gdpr  # or: none, hipaa, financial
  use_case: customer_support

slo:
  availability:
    target_percent: 99.5
    window_days: 30
  latency:
    p95_ms: 3000
    window_days: 30
  error_rate:
    threshold_percent: 1.0
  llm_cost:
    budget_usd_per_1k_sessions: 12.00
A SaaS company onboards three new agent teams per quarter. Before introducing onboarding standards, each team's traces used different attribute names for the same concepts (
), making cross-team dashboards impossible. Two services went to production without error rate alerts configured, causing a 45-minute MTTR on incidents.

After introducing the five-step onboarding flow with automated CI lint gates, all new services ship with consistent attribute naming, at least three SLO declarations, and PagerDuty routing configured. Average onboarding time is 2 days from registration to go-live. The span schema linter catches attribute naming errors and PII leaks in CI, before they reach the compliance archive.
Making onboarding a one-time gate rather than a living standard — teams that onboard before a new required attribute is added will never add it unless re-validation is enforced.
Running the schema linter only in staging where trace volume is too low to catch missing attributes on infrequent code paths.
Requiring too many attributes at once — a 40-item required attribute list creates such friction that teams bypass the process.
No self-service portal — teams must open tickets to check their lint status, slowing iteration and creating platform team bottlenecks.
Not versioning the attribute schema — changing a required attribute name without a deprecation period breaks existing tenants.
Keep the required attribute list small (8–12 attributes) and truly universal; domain-specific attributes should be recommended, not required.
Enforce re-validation of existing tenants quarterly via a scheduled CI job on all registered services, not just at initial onboarding.
Provide a local linter that developers can run before pushing to CI, reducing feedback loop from minutes to seconds.
Automate namespace provisioning from the service registry — remove human steps between registration approval and platform access.
Publish clear error messages from the linter that link to documentation explaining how to fix each failure — "Missing attribute: service.team" should link to an example.

The onboarding process itself must scale with the number of new services per quarter. At 3 services per quarter, manual review is fine. At 20 per quarter, every step should be self-service and automated. The CI linter should complete in under 30 seconds to not block developer workflows; achieve this by linting a sample of 100 spans from staging rather than the full trace volume. The service registry provisioning controller should reconcile new registrations within 5 minutes of PR merge.
Provision service keys from registry YAML

Required attribute schemas must be versioned to allow backward-compatible evolution. When the platform team adds a new required attribute, existing tenants must have a deprecation period (typically one or two quarters) before the new attribute becomes mandatory in CI. Use a

field in the service registry YAML to track which schema version a service is validated against. The CI linter reads this version and applies the corresponding schema rules, allowing gradual migration rather than forcing all tenants to update simultaneously.

# Schema versioning in service registry
service:
  name: my-agent
  team: platform
  schema_version: "2.1"  # must be >= minimum required version

# Changelog:
# 2.0: added service.team (required)
# 2.1: added compliance.scope (required for regulated services)
# 2.2 [beta]: adding llm.model (required for LLM-using services)
#             enforcement date: 2026-Q3

The most security-sensitive onboarding check is the banned attribute pattern scan, which prevents PII from entering the shared trace backend. This scan must run in CI where access to the staging trace dump is controlled; it should not rely on developer self-attestation. For regulated services, the compliance scope attribute must be validated against an approved list of scopes, not accepted as free-form text, to prevent misclassification (e.g., a service tagging itself as

Publish a public readiness dashboard inside Grafana that shows the onboarding status of all registered services: which schema version they are validated against, which required attributes are missing, when their last successful lint run was, and whether their SLO declarations are up to date. This dashboard serves both team leads ("is my service compliant?") and platform engineers ("which tenants need nudging before the next schema version enforcement date?"). Automate weekly Slack digests to team leads whose services are out of compliance, reducing the need for platform team follow-ups.
The readiness dashboard also supports audits: a compliance auditor can see at a glance that all agent services processing HIPAA data have

in their schema, that the lint gate passed as recently as the last deployment, and that their retention policy is configured correctly. This turns the onboarding process from an internal engineering practice into a demonstrable audit control.
Onboarding standards protect both the shared platform (schema consistency, cardinality control) and the tenant teams (SLOs defined, alerts wired before go-live).
Automated CI lint gates are the enforcement mechanism — manual review does not scale past a few services per quarter.
Keep required attributes minimal (8–12); recommended attributes can be richer but should not block onboarding.
Re-validate existing tenants on a regular schedule — onboarding is a living standard, not a one-time gate.
Automate namespace and alert routing provisioning from the service registry; eliminate human steps between registration approval and platform access.
The PII and compliance scope scan is the highest-value security check — validate it in CI, not through self-attestation.
